program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_desc: Reading a recovery plan that delegates the fixes after the failed launch -- the causative (have/get something done; have someone do; get someone to do) -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- The Recovery Plan -- Delegating the Fixes
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Lesson 16 -- The Recovery Plan

listenings.text: Izabel, it is Marcus, your operations partner, with a progress note on the Halo recovery before the review. The turnaround is under way. We are having the firmware rewritten by an external security firm this week, and we will get the whole system retested before a single unit ships. On the process side, I have had the release checklist overhauled from scratch, because it was the old checklist that let the defect through. Here is the change that matters most: every fix now has a named owner. Nobody signs off on a shipment until quality has certified it, and any missed date is escalated to me the same day. Where we cannot rectify a gap in-house, we bring in a third party rather than let it drift. I am not going to pretend it was painless, but we are getting the whole organization up to speed fast. Call me before the review and I will walk you through the owners.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Sofia, your operations lead, with the remediation update you asked for. Let me be precise. The firmware is the priority, so we are having it rewritten by specialists, and I am getting an independent analyst to rebuild the forecast model, because the unrealistic forecast is what caused the excess stock. On supply, I have had the contract reworked so that a late shipment is flagged in writing and escalated the same day. The biggest change is ownership. In the past, a fault was noticed and nobody took ownership; now each item has one accountable owner who must get the fix done and report every Thursday. Remediation is not open-ended; every task has a date. If we have the right work done by the right people, this setback becomes a turnaround. I will bring the full owner list on Thursday.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a recovery that reverses a failing situation
a thorough repair or redesign of something
to correct a mistake or a fault
to pass a problem to a higher level for action
required to explain results and answer for them
the work of fixing a defect or problem
to give something final, formal approval
an outside company or person, not you or the client

paras: After the post-mortem, this plan sets out how AVRT will rescue the Halo line. The message is simple: we will not fix everything ourselves; we will have each problem handled by the right specialist and hold one owner accountable for each fix. The firmware defect comes first. We are having the code rewritten by an external security firm, and we will get the whole system retested before any customer sees it. This is the turnaround, and it starts with a full overhaul of the release process.

Each workstream has a clear handover. On forecasting, we are getting an independent analyst to rebuild the model, because it was the unrealistic forecast that caused the excess stock. On supply, we have had the contract reworked so that a missed date is escalated the same day, and we have asked the vendor to flag every delay in writing. Nobody signs off on a shipment now until quality has certified it. Where the team cannot rectify a gap in-house, we bring in a third party rather than let the problem drift.

What makes this plan different is ownership. In the past, a fault was noticed and nobody took ownership; this time each item has a named owner who must get the fix done and report on it every Thursday. Remediation is not optional, and it is not open-ended: every task has a date. The reason we will recover is not luck; it is that we are having the right work done by the right people, and we are getting the whole organization up to speed on the new process. Read fairly, the plan turns a setback into a turnaround.
reading.source: Adapted from an internal AVRT recovery plan

reading.prompt: What is the main purpose of this plan?
choices: To explain in detail why the Halo launch failed.
To set out how AVRT will fix the Halo problems by delegating each fix to the right owner and specialist.
To announce that the Halo line is being cancelled.

items: The plan says AVRT will fix every problem with its own in-house team.
'We will not fix everything ourselves; we will have each problem handled by the right specialist.'
An external security firm is rewriting the firmware code.
'We are having the code rewritten by an external security firm.'
Quality must certify a shipment before anyone signs off on it.
'Nobody signs off on a shipment now until quality has certified it.'
Each remediation task is open-ended, with no deadline.
'Remediation is not optional, and it is not open-ended: every task has a date.'

parts: by a security firm, and we will
off on a shipment until quality certifies it.

gapfill.text: The causative means you ARRANGE for someone else to do the work. Have / get + something + past participle focuses on the RESULT: 'We had the firmware rewritten' (someone else wrote it, not us). Have + someone + base verb, or get + someone + to + base, names the DOER: 'We had the vendor rebuild it' / 'We got the vendor to rebuild it'. Word order matters: have + OBJECT + past participle, never 'have rewritten the firmware' (that means you did it yourself).

bank.label: Useful language for delegating the fixes
items: We are having the firmware rewritten by a specialist.
We got the process audited by a third party.
We had the vendor flag every delay in writing.
We will get an independent analyst to rebuild the forecast.
Nobody signs off on it until quality certifies it.
Each owner must get the fix done by Thursday.

lf.title: Causative: which pattern?
items: the code rewritten by a firm.
the vendor to flag every delay.

lf.text: Two patterns. Result: have / get + OBJECT + past participle ('We had it fixed'). The doer: have + person + base, or get + person + to + base ('We had them fix it' / 'We got them to fix it'). Use 'get ... to' when there is effort or persuasion; use plain 'have ... do' for a neutral arrangement.

items: Delegate the firmware fix with 'have something done': the code, an external firm.
Delegate the forecast with 'get someone to do something': an analyst, rebuild the model.
State the quality rule with the causative: quality certifies, then someone signs off.
Explain your recovery plan for one real problem at work, using at least two causative structures.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = having, 2 = rewritten, 3 = get, 4 = to, 5 = signs.
Scenario 1: 'We are having the code rewritten by an external firm.'
Scenario 2: 'We got an independent analyst to rebuild the model.'
Scenario 3: 'We have quality certify every shipment before anyone signs off on it.'
answerkey.note: Result: have / get + object + past participle. The doer: have + person + base, or get + person + to + base.
